summary: Daily broad HN comment scan; 10 concrete products, references, and creative projects selected after full reachable-comment coverage.
telegram_footer: 10 items surfaced | 25/25 stories scraped | history + run artifact updated

items.name: QuadRF
items.source_story_title: QuadRF can spot drones and see WiFi through my wall
items.summary: A 4×4 MIMO software-defined radio platform for visualizing RF sources and beamforming.
items.why_included: An unusually accessible phased-array SDR kit with concrete maker and engineering appeal.
items.evidence: Commenters highlighted its hobbyist-friendly packaging, Wi-Fi RF visualization, and open-source customization.

items.name: War Atlas
items.source_story_title: War Atlas: An interactive cartography of every named war in human history
items.summary: An interactive map and timeline of named wars across human history.
items.why_included: A compact, explorable historical reference with broad serendipity value.
items.evidence: HN readers praised the interactive concept while discussing its cartographic choices and historical coverage.

items.name: Combustion Lab
items.summary: A browser-based internal-combustion-engine simulator with live mechanical diagrams.
items.why_included: A visually strong educational simulation for understanding engine motion and cycles.
items.evidence: Commenters called the live diagrams impressive, while noting that physical validation still matters.

items.name: Anki
items.summary: A spaced-repetition flashcard app for durable recall across languages, medicine, and technical subjects.
items.why_included: A well-established learning tool backed by unusually practical discussion of how to use it well.
items.evidence: HN users recommended sentence mining, custom cards, and active-recall practice rather than rote cramming.

items.name: Memory Machines
items.summary: Research and tools for designing better spaced-repetition learning prompts.
items.why_included: Useful companion material for anyone trying to make flashcards less mechanical and more effective.
items.evidence: A commenter recommended its report by Ozzie Kirkby and Andy Matuschak for thinking about modern SRS practice.

items.name: Lost Worlds
items.summary: Patrick Wyman’s history book reexamining how humans moved from foraging to early states.
items.why_included: A fresh, concrete reading recommendation for people drawn to the Bronze Age Collapse discussion.
items.evidence: HN commenters recommended it for challenging the familiar linear story of farming, cities, and states.

items.name: Frigade Skills
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Reverse-engineering web apps into agent tools
items.summary: An agent layer that learns in-app actions and lets assistants execute them client-side.
items.why_included: A concrete approach to turning web-app workflows into usable agent capabilities.
items.evidence: The maker said training runs in demo or staging environments and tool calls execute in the client.

items.name: Fading Maize
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Reviving my 2001 college band with AI
items.summary: A project revisiting early-2000s band recordings through AI-assisted musical reinterpretation.
items.why_included: A thoughtful creative experiment that documents both the promise and limitations of AI music.
items.evidence: The creators described it as a nostalgic collaboration, while commenters debated the smoother AI-produced sound.

items.name: Neralie
items.source_story_title: Alternate Clock Designs and Time Systems
items.summary: A decimal-time format with 1,000 beats and one million pulses per day.
items.why_included: A distinctive, usable alternate-time design with an open implementation.
items.evidence: An HN commenter recommended Neralie as a favorite base-10 clock and linked its specification and source.

items.name: DayClocks
items.source_story_title: Alternate Clock Designs and Time Systems
items.summary: A seven-segment clock that prominently displays the current day of the week.
items.why_included: A simple physical product that solves an ordinary, surprisingly useful orientation problem.
items.evidence: A commenter called it a favorite retirement gift and reported later thanks from recipients who used it.
